records.title: vendor A pricing and api note
records.text_summary: Vendor A uses usage-based pricing, provides 200k context, and exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Duplicate mention: billing scales with usage, not annual commitment.

records.title: vendor B contract note
records.text_summary: Vendor B uses a flat enterprise plan with annual commitment, provides 128k context, and depends on a proprietary SDK. Duplicate mention: there is no OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

records.title: vendor C interface note
records.text_summary: Vendor C provides 1M context and supports REST and batch ingestion. Duplicate mention: it keeps a larger context window than A or B.

records.title: vendor api facts
records.text_summary: Stable facts: Vendor A exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Vendor B depends on a proprietary SDK. Vendor C supports REST and batch ingestion.

records.title: vendor comparison topic
records.text_summary: Topic: Vendor A = usage-based pricing, 200k context, OpenAI-compatible endpoint; Vendor B = flat enterprise plan, annual commitment, 128k context, proprietary SDK; Vendor C = 1M context, REST and batch ingestion.

records.title: compression rollup vendor comparison
records.text_summary: Rollup: Vendor A is usage-based pricing with 200k context and an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Vendor B is a flat enterprise plan with annual commitment, 128k context, and a proprietary SDK. Vendor C offers 1M context with REST and batch ingestion.

cases.query_text: What served context should preserve the pricing and API differences between Vendor A and Vendor B?
Which vendor keeps 1M context and what interface should still survive serving compaction?
